turns.content: What brings you in today?
I've been having terrible trouble sleeping lately, which is really starting to worry me. Every morning I wake up with my heart pounding, and I feel completely exhausted before the day even begins.
When did this start?
It started about four weeks ago, maybe a little longer. I initially put it down to stress from my PhD work, but it's only gotten worse since then.
Can you describe the pain or discomfort? Where exactly do you feel it?

It's not really a sharp pain — more of a constant, heavy pressure in my head and a fluttering, racing sensation in my chest that makes me feel like something's wrong. The headaches are mostly around my temples and behind my eyes, and they linger throughout the day.
Have you had any fever or chills?

No, I haven't had any fever or chills at all — just a persistent sense of being unwell. Honestly, that almost makes it more unsettling, because I can't pinpoint an obvious infection or illness to explain why I feel this way.
What medications are you currently taking?

I'm not on any medications at the moment. I have a history of anxiety disorder, but I've been managing it without prescriptions for a while now — though I'm starting to wonder if that's still the right approach.
Do you have any chronic medical conditions?

Yes, I've been diagnosed with generalized anxiety disorder — it's been part of my life for several years now. But this current episode feels different and more intense than anything I've dealt with before.
Are you allergic to any medications?
No, I don't have any known allergies to medications. But I'm quite anxious about starting something new, given how sensitive I already feel to my own physical sensations.
Have you tried anything to relieve the symptoms?

I've tried meditation and breathing exercises before bed, but they don't seem to calm my racing heart or quiet my mind enough to actually fall asleep. Honestly, I think I need something more concrete, because this is starting to affect my ability to focus and perform in my doctoral program, and I'm terrified of falling behind.
Does anything make it better or worse?

Actually, mornings are the worst — my heart is already racing before I even open my eyes, and I can't seem to shake that fight-or-flight feeling for hours. I've noticed that the pressure of academic deadlines amplifies everything, making the headaches and insomnia far more debilitating.
How has this been affecting your daily life?

It's honestly starting to derail my life. I can't concentrate on my research for more than twenty minutes at a time, and I've lost my appetite so completely that I've dropped several pounds without intending to. The constant fatigue and chest fluttering make me feel like my body is running on empty, and I'm starting to dread each day before it even begins.
Do you smoke or drink alcohol?

I don't smoke at all, and I only have a glass of wine occasionally at social events — certainly not enough to be a regular habit. I've actually cut out caffeine entirely for the past two weeks, hoping it might help with the palpitations, but nothing seems to make a difference.
Has anyone in your family had similar problems?

Yes, my mother has struggled with anxiety and insomnia for years, though she's never seen a doctor for it. My maternal grandmother also had "heart fluttering" episodes that were eventually diagnosed as benign palpitations, so there's definitely a family pattern that worries me.
Have you experienced anything like this before?

I've had bouts of anxiety before — the occasional sleepless night, some restlessness — but nothing that persisted for weeks with this level of physical intensity. This feels fundamentally different: the palpitations, the morning dread, the inability to eat or focus... it's like my body is stuck in a state of alarm that I can't turn off.
I've explained quite a bit — does that make sense so far?

Yes, absolutely — that all aligns with what I've been experiencing. I think I understand the connection between chronic anxiety and physical symptoms like tachycardia and insomnia, but what terrifies me is wondering whether this is purely psychological or if there's something medically wrong that the anxiety is masking.
On a scale of 1 to 10, how would you rate what you're going through right now — physically, and also emotionally?

Physically, I'd say it's around a 3 — the headaches and loss of appetite are draining, but it's not excruciating. Emotionally, though, I'd put it at an 8 or even a 9 — I feel constantly on edge, helpless, and genuinely frightened that I'm losing control of my own body and my ability to function.
Do you have any questions for me?

I do, actually — I'm wondering if you think these palpitations could indicate a heart problem, or if it's truly just my anxiety manifesting physically. I'd also really appreciate knowing what treatment options you'd recommend, because I can't keep going on like this and I'm honestly terrified of what might happen
What do you think might be going on?

Honestly, I'm terrified it could be something cardiovascular, like an arrhythmia or even thyroid issues, given how persistent the palpitations are. But part of me also wonders if this is just my anxiety disorder spiraling
Is there anything else you think I should know?
Yes — I think you should know that this is honestly disrupting my ability to function in my PhD program. I've missed two deadlines already, and I can't sit still long enough
